Most of our blog posts show what ONE Teneo agent does.

This one shows what happens when you chain TWO of them into an actual workflow.

Our Head of Marketing @Sarut0biSasuke  runs meeting prep using Hermes from @NousResearch 

- Every 10 minutes it checks Google calendar
- For any attendee it doesn't know, it calls the Teneo LinkedIn Agent (enrich_url <url>) for who they are 
- Then the Teneo X Agent to find their project's X account
- Then it drops a one-page brief before the meeting starts.

The cost: 

LinkedIn enrichment costs $0.006/query, X context from $0.002/query, both settled in USDC over x402 on Base. 

For ten meetings a week, three attendees each, it will set you back about a dollar a month. 

Sales Navigator is $100+/month and still makes you do the reading yourself.

The full post covers the workflow end to end:

https://t.co/jroR40XxCJ

EVERYONE AND THEIR GRANDMA HAS BEEN TALKING ABOUT LOOP ENGINEERING BUT NOBODY IS SHOWING YOU HOW TO ACTUALLY BUILD A LOOP.

Here is the before and after. Before loop engineering you prompt, test, prompt, test, prompt, test-every step manual, nothing happens when you're not at your computer, you're the bottleneck.

After loop engineering you spend a few minutes telling the agent your ideas, it goes in a loop all day building, reviewing, testing itself autonomously, and at the end you quickly click merge.

The loop has three skills: spec, build, review.

Spec interviews you until it fully understands your idea, generates detailed issues with acceptance criteria, and sends them to Linear.

Build pulls those issues and builds them out.

Review opens browsers, clicks around, tests everything, and spins up pull requests with preview branches.

Run slash loop 5 min slash him build and slash loop 5 min slash him review in separate chats.

Every five minutes the agent builds and reviews autonomously all day while you're away. When review passes, the agent messages you in Slack with a preview link. React with a rocket emoji and it merges automatically.

Your work goes from sitting at your computer all day to coming in the morning, giving ideas, and coming back at night to hit merge on everything.

95% of the vibe coding work is now automated. This works with Claude Code, Codeex, any tool that lets you build custom skills.

No gatekeeping. Block off half an hour, set this up, and you are ahead of 99.999999% of people.

Oracle cut its workforce by 21,000, and now faces a $7 billion hurdle for an OpenAI data center.

Oracle is building a nearly one-gigawatt campus in Wisconsin as part of its $300 billion cloud deal with OpenAI.

But state regulators want Oracle to guarantee more than $7 billion of the power infrastructure built for the project. Oracle’s credit rating has fallen to BBB-, below the required level. A letter of credit could cost the company over $100 million every year.

While Oracle continues to lose value, SpaceX is gaining importance as a hyperscaler.

🚨 Would you be comfortable paying extra for a home service if the recorded footage from your home was later used to train AI models?

A new opt-in service model is introducing exactly that, with workers earning higher pay while anonymized recordings contribute to AI training.

It raises bigger questions around privacy, data ownership, and whether service bookings are quietly becoming AI datasets.

Every inch of this train becomes a weapon when survival is the only option.

prompt

Part 1 (0–15 Seconds)

A cinematic 15-second realistic action sequence inside an empty modern subway train car at night. Fluorescent ceiling lights cast harsh, cool white reflections across polished gray floors, brushed metal walls, silver handrails, and blue fabric seats. The train moves at high speed, creating subtle vibrations as overhead straps sway naturally.

A fierce East Asian woman in her late 20s with dark hair secured in a tight high bun wears sleek black tactical gear consisting of a fitted long-sleeve compression top, cargo pants, combat boots, fingerless gloves, and tactical harness straps. She faces four heavily armed male assassins dressed in black tactical uniforms with balaclavas.

The sequence opens with a low-angle tracking shot as the assassins rapidly surround her. She instantly drops into a low combat stance, slides across the polished floor beneath a sweeping attack, and uses the momentum to sweep an opponent's legs. She springs to her feet, delivering rapid elbow strikes and spinning back kicks while weaving between vertical poles and seat rows. One attacker charges from behind; she vaults over a subway seat, plants both feet on the seatback, and launches herself into a flying kick that knocks him backward into the train doors. Another assassin swings a baton, but she ducks, rolls beneath the strike, grabs an overhead handrail, and swings through the aisle to deliver a powerful mid-air double kick that sends two attackers crashing into opposite rows of seats. Fast handheld camera, dynamic tracking shots, Dutch angles, realistic impacts, gritty high-contrast desaturated color grading, movie-quality choreography inspired by Atomic Blonde and John Wick. No music, only heavy breathing, punches, impacts, footsteps, metallic rattles, and train ambience.

Part 2 (15–30 Seconds) — Continuation

Continuation of the exact same subway fight with identical character appearance, clothing, lighting, camera style, and environment. The East Asian woman maintains the same hairstyle, tactical outfit, and fluid fighting style as the battle intensifies inside the moving subway car.

One assassin pulls a combat knife while another rushes with a metal pipe. She sidesteps at the last second, forcing them to collide before countering with rapid knee strikes and a spinning hook kick. She dives low between seat rows, crawls beneath grabbing hands, then explodes upward into a devastating uppercut that sends one attacker crashing into a glass partition. The tempered glass shatters realistically as fragments scatter across the floor. She grabs a dropped backpack and hurls it into another attacker's face before using a vertical pole to spin around with incredible momentum, delivering a flying roundhouse kick that throws the final assassin through a subway window. Glass bursts outward in slow, realistic fragments while cold night air rushes into the train.

The final moments show the floor covered with shattered glass, abandoned weapons, backpacks, and defeated assassins. She stands breathing heavily in the center aisle, adjusting her fingerless glove before calmly walking toward the subway doors as sparks flicker from damaged lighting overhead. Dynamic low-angle tracking, close-up impact shots, handheld cinematography, realistic physics, gritty desaturated color grade, razor-sharp action, cinematic lighting, no music, only realistic fight sounds, shattering glass, metallic impacts, train rumble, and heavy breathing.

👀

It’s worse than that.

This is person, not just vehicle level, persistence. The sensors capture Bluetooth, Wi-Fi, RFID, tire-pressure monitors, key fobs, wearables, earbuds, and even pet microchips. AI then treats the recurring bundles of these signals as a durable signature. It’s like the stream I did where I talked about ways they can identify you even with a VPN because they can bundle other anonymous data points about how you move your mouse etc that together paints an identifiable picture.

Change plates, switch cars, walk past the pole or ride a bike, the same bundle can still identify you. The plate reader becomes optional at that point.

Silent, no hastle rollout on existing infrastructure. Agencies can enable the capability without new poles, fresh contracts, or fresh public votes. It’s just a new feature on the already normalized tech that everyone in government especially law enforcement wants.

Passive mass collection + relationship inference - remember the contact tracing they wouldn’t shut up about during covid? This does that. Everything that radiates nearby is logged and stored permanently. Recurring co-location tracks who regularly travels or spends time together, creating an inferred social graph without anyone ever knowing it. Collection happens silently 24/7 regardless.

This changes flock cameras from “where did this car go?” to “where did this unique device constellation go, who else was near it, and what pattern does that form?” 

But somehow we can’t deport people. I wonder who this could be for…

🔥 RWA has quickly become one of Web3's fastest-growing sectors. But we're only at the beginning.

We're joining @Pumpkin_global for #PumpkinTalk #24 to explore what comes next.

Tokenized Treasuries, private credit, equities — traditional assets are moving on-chain faster than ever. 📈

The conversation will get into the specifics: Which asset classes are actually ready for prime time, and which are still too complex to tokenize at scale? What's really holding back liquidity — is it infrastructure, regulation, or simply a lack of institutional conviction? And with AI Agents becoming more autonomous by the day, will they be the ones driving real demand for RWAs, or is that scenario still years away from playing out?

Beyond the headlines, we'll also dig into the quieter but equally important shifts: How are compliance frameworks evolving to keep up with tokenized assets? And which business models are most likely to emerge when AI, RWAs, and stablecoins finally converge?

🚨 The biggest mistake perp traders make isn't picking the wrong trade.

It's failing to execute the right one.

Markets don't wait.

You sleep. You work. You miss entries.

That's why I'm paying more attention to execution infrastructure than another trading dashboard.

One project I've been testing is @farsideapp.

Instead of watching charts all day, you build your strategy once and let automation handle the execution.

Right now it supports @HyperliquidX and @dYdX, with features built around disciplined trading rather than emotional decision-making.

Here's what stood out to me:

• Non-custodial automated execution
• AI-assisted risk supervision
• Grid bots that take just minutes to configure
• Paper trading and backtesting before risking capital
• Telegram alerts so you stay informed without living on TradingView

The interesting part isn't just the automation.

It's how Farside is connecting research → strategy → execution into one workflow.

Most trading platforms stop after giving you charts.

Execution is still left to you.

Farside is trying to solve that gap.

They've also launched their public points program:

• Earn 1 point for every $100 in trading volume
• Leaderboard goes live so you can track your ranking as you trade

If perpetuals continue leading this cycle, I think the infrastructure built around them deserves just as much attention as the exchanges themselves.

Still early, but this is one I'll be watching closely as the Hyperliquid ecosystem keeps expanding.

Try it here ⤵️
https://t.co/w2kUESeLVX
